text: The surgeon probes the external opening of the fistula and traces the tract to the internal opening. A retractor is placed in the anal canal, and a metal probe is left in the fistula to aid in subsequent dissection.
The surgeon makes an incision at the anocutaneous border, revealing the intersphincteric space and identifying the intersphincteric part of the fistula tract.
The surgeon dissects the fistula tract adjacent to the internal and external sphincter, followed by ligation of the tract to prevent further communication between the sphincters.
The surgeon cuts the fistula tract between the previously placed ligatures, completing the dissection of the tract.
The surgeon inserts the fistulascope through the external opening, exploring the fistula tract for any additional secondary branches.
The surgeon performs fulguration of the fistula, using electrical energy to ablate the tissue, followed by curettage to remove necrotic tissue from the fistula tract.
The surgical wound is irrigated with hydrogen peroxide and saline solution, visibly cleansing the area.
The surgeon closes the space along the endocutaneous border using resorbable interrupted sutures, completing the incision closure.
The surgeon makes an incision on the contralateral side of the anacutaneous border, revealing the internal sphincter beneath the tissue.
The surgeon performs a small sphincterotomy to relieve tension in the anal structure, using precise incisions to modify the sphincter muscle.
The surgeon performs a sphincterotomy on the side opposite the fistula, making an incision away from the visible inflammatory process.
The surgeon closes the wound using resorbable interrupted sutures, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
The surgeon excises the external fistula opening, removing the affected tissue to facilitate healing.
